topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mail slot rattled, a dull, irritating sound that always preceded another piece of unsolicited junk. I pulled the flap open with a sigh that tasted of stale coffee. There it was: a thick, cream-colored card, completely blank save for a hastily scrawled 'Happy Birthday.' My jaw tightened. I didn't even bother to unfold it completely, just held the flimsy paper between my thumb and forefinger, inspecting the cheap, glittery embossing. It felt like an insult, a clumsy attempt at connection from someone I didn't even know. I let out a short, sharp exhale, crumpling the paper into a tight, irregular ball before dropping it into the bin. It was pointless, this whole ritual of being acknowledged by strangers.

[story 2]

I stared at the card under the harsh fluorescent light of the hallway. It was addressed to 'Recipient,' a vague, impersonal label. The handwriting on the greeting was looping and overly cheerful, utterly devoid of substance. A muscle ticked in my jaw. Why would some random person feel the need to waste paper and ink on something so utterly generic? I picked it up, the texture thin and flimsy against my palm. I turned it over, noticing the faint smudge near the corner. A slow, deliberate motion drew my hand toward the wastebasket. With a decisive snap, I tossed it in, the sound a small, satisfying punctuation mark to the day’s general sense of irritation.

[story 3]

The mailbox overflowed, a testament to the sheer volume of meaningless correspondence flooding my doorstep. Amidst the glossy flyers and bills, a pale piece of cardstock caught my eye. An unsigned birthday greeting. I felt a sudden, hot flush rise up my neck. I leaned closer, my expression hardening into something distinctly unimpressed. The message—'Wishing you the best on your day'—seemed to mock the very idea of genuine sentiment. I didn't want it. I didn't want the implication that someone outside my orbit was invested in my existence. I crushed the card in my fist until the paper protested with a faint, dry rustle, then dropped the debris without looking.
